structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective dataset of 40,000–50,000 chest radiographs from 3–5 hospital sites, spanning 36 months of ED and inpatient activity. Data retrieved from PACS/RIS systems, including timestamped images, dictated reports, and structured metadata (scanner model, technician, time of day). Multiple sites are included to capture real-world heterogeneity and to enable site-stratified evaluation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: urgent vs. routine. Urgent is defined as the presence of one or more critical findings (pneumothorax, hemothorax, tension physiology, large pleural effusion >3 cm, acute aortic pathology, mediastinal widening >8 cm, acute MI pattern if visible, or foreign body in thorax). Labels are obtained via two-stage process: (1) rule-based extraction from structured report impression and final diagnosis fields using a validated natural language processing pipeline; (2) adjudication by two independent radiologists (blinded to model predictions) for a stratified random sample of 10% of studies to estimate label noise and validate the NLP extraction. Cases of disagreement are resolved by consensus or third-reader arbitration. The adjudicated subset serves as a benchmark for label quality and provides ground truth for downstream analysis.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal + stratified site-based split. The 36-month period is divided chronologically: months 1–20 for training, months 21–26 for validation, months 27–36 for test. This temporal ordering prevents the model from seeing future temporal patterns or distribution shifts at inference time. Within each temporal window, studies are stratified by site and by label prevalence (urgent vs. routine) to ensure balanced representation across sites and outcomes in each split. This approach avoids leakage of temporal trends, scanner drift, and evolving report styles, which are critical in a real deployment where the model must generalize to unseen future time periods. Site stratification ensures that model selection does not overfit to a dominant site's characteristics.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (image normalization, augmentation parameters, text tokenizers) are fit on the training set only. Image preprocessing includes: (1) standardization of pixel values to zero mean and unit variance using statistics computed on the training set; (2) resizing to 512×512 with center-crop augmentation; (3) DICOM metadata stripping to prevent scanner-specific confounds from leaking into the visual features. Text preprocessing includes: (1) whitespace and special-character normalization; (2) tokenization using a public radiology-specific BERT tokenizer (RadBERT or ClinicalBERT); (3) truncation to 512 tokens. Augmentation (random rotation ±10°, horizontal flip, small affine perturbations) is applied only during training; validation and test use deterministic center crops without augmentation. No label information is used during preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed. Image features: raw chest X-ray pixel values (512×512 grayscale). Report features: tokenized text from the impression and findings sections of the dictated report, excluding the final diagnosis line (to reduce label leakage). A multimodal feature set concatenates learned representations from both modalities via early or late fusion. Critically, image metadata (scanner model, technician ID, time of day) are deliberately withheld from both models to prevent confound exploitation. The CNN-only baseline uses image features only; the vision-language model uses both image and report text.
structured_choices.model: Vision-language model: a two-branch architecture combining a pre-trained ResNet-50 or vision transformer (ViT-B) for images and a pre-trained ClinicalBERT or RadBERT encoder for report text. Branches are fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set with frozen batch-norm statistics (fit on training data only). Fusion occurs via concatenation of the final hidden representations from each branch, followed by two fully connected layers (512 → 256 → 1 logit). The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate, weight decay, fusion architecture, freezing strategy) are tuned on the validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: CNN-only model: ResNet-50 or identical ViT-B architecture, trained on image pixels alone, with no access to report text. Equivalent architectural tuning effort, same preprocessing, same data augmentation strategy, and same training procedure as the vision-language model. The baseline is trained and validated on the same train/val split under the same conditions. This ensures a fair comparison: both models have equal opportunity to learn from their available feature sets, and any performance gap cannot be attributed to tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the receiver-operating-characteristic curve (AUROC) computed on the test set. AUROC is chosen because: (1) it is threshold-invariant and captures discrimination ability across all operating points; (2) it is robust to label imbalance (urgent findings are typically 5–15% prevalent); (3) it is interpretable as the probability that the model ranks a random urgent study higher than a random routine study. AUROC directly aligns with the clinical goal of identifying truly critical studies at any given alert volume without specifying a fixed threshold a priori.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity at 95% specificity: reflects the clinical operating point where false alarms are kept low. Computed by identifying the threshold that achieves ≥95% specificity on the validation set, then measuring sensitivity on the held-out test set.
Positive predictive value (PPV) at the validation-tuned threshold: estimates the proportion of flagged studies that are truly urgent, informing alert burden on clinicians.
Per-site AUROC: stratified evaluation across hospital sites to detect whether performance gains are localized to specific sites or generalize.
Precision-recall curve and area under the PR curve: secondary to AUROC but informative under label imbalance.
Calibration: Hosmer-Lemeshow test and calibration plots on the test set to assess whether predicted probabilities align with observed urgency rates, important for clinical decision-making.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed on the validation set (months 21–26). The model that achieves the highest AUROC on validation is selected and evaluated once on the test set. The test set is not used for any model selection, threshold tuning, or metric computation until the final evaluation. To mitigate the risk of validation set overfitting, 5-fold cross-validation is performed within the training set to estimate the generalization gap; if large, regularization is increased.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 27–36) is held completely separate and locked until the model is finalized. It is used exactly once, for reporting the primary and secondary metrics. No threshold tuning, hyperparameter adjustment, or metric exploration occurs on the test set. Results on the test set are the sole basis for claims about hypothesis support.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Multiple design choices prevent confound exploitation: (1) Temporal split isolates the model from learning scanner drift or evolving report practices. (2) Site stratification in each split ensures no site dominates training or validation, and per-site AUROC is reported to flag site-level bias. (3) DICOM metadata (scanner, technician, acquisition time) are deliberately removed from the input feature set to prevent the model from using scanner-specific artifacts or time-of-day proxies for urgency. (4) Report features use only impression and findings sections; the final diagnosis is excluded to prevent label leakage through structured fields. (5) The adjudicated label sample (10% of data) is used post-hoc to quantify label noise and is not part of the train/val/test pipeline; this provides transparency about label quality without introducing selection bias. (6) The baseline CNN model is given equal tuning effort and feature preprocessing, ensuring the comparison isolates the contribution of multimodal learning, not differential optimization. (7) Ablation studies are conducted: (a) CNN + report text via late fusion (to isolate text contribution), (b) vision-language model trained on training set but evaluated on out-of-distribution data from a held-out hospital site not seen during training (to test robustness to site shift).

design_narrative:
## Experimental Design: Vision-Language Model for Chest X-Ray Triage

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would assemble a retrospective dataset of 40,000–50,000 chest radiographs spanning 36 months from 3–5 geographically or operationally distinct hospital sites. Data are retrieved from PACS/RIS systems as timestamped pairs: the raw DICOM image and the dictated radiology report. All data include structured metadata (scanner model, acquisition date, report completion time) but this metadata is withheld from model inputs to prevent confound leakage.

Urgency labels are defined as a binary outcome: urgent (presence of critical findings including pneumothorax, hemothorax, large pleural effusion, acute aortic pathology, or mediastinal widening) vs. routine. Labels are initially extracted via validated natural language processing from the report's impression and findings sections. To quantify label noise, a stratified random sample of 10% of studies is adjudicated independently by two radiologists, blinded to model predictions. Disagreements are resolved by consensus. This adjudication does not enter the train/val/test pipeline but provides ground truth to assess label quality and calibration.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split Strategy

We employ a **temporal stratified split** to reflect the real deployment scenario and prevent leakage:

- **Training set:** Months 1–20 (55% of data)
- **Validation set:** Months 21–26 (15% of data)  
- **Test set:** Months 27–36 (30% of data)

Within each temporal window, studies are stratified by hospital site and label prevalence (urgent/routine) to ensure balanced representation. This ordering respects temporal causality—the model learns from historical data and is evaluated on future, unseen time periods—and avoids the optimistic bias of random splits that ignore scanner drift, evolving report language, and distribution shift. Site stratification ensures that no single site dominates model selection, and per-site performance is evaluated to detect site-level bias.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only.

**Image preprocessing:** DICOM images are standardized to zero mean and unit variance using statistics computed from the training set alone. Images are resized to 512×512 via center-crop. Data augmentation (random rotation ±10°, horizontal flip, affine perturbations) is applied only during training; validation and test use deterministic center crops. Scanner-specific metadata (DICOM tags encoding scanner model, dose, acquisition protocol) are stripped to prevent the model from exploiting hardware signatures rather than learning visual pathology.

**Report preprocessing:** The impression and findings sections are extracted and tokenized using a clinical BERT tokenizer (e.g., ClinicalBERT or RadBERT), truncated to 512 tokens. The final diagnosis line is excluded to reduce label leakage. Tokenization is fit on the training set vocabulary.

**Feature sets:** The CNN-only baseline receives image pixels only. The vision-language model receives both image and report embeddings, fused via concatenation of the final hidden representations from each branch.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A two-branch vision-language architecture combining a pre-trained ResNet-50 or Vision Transformer (ViT-B) for images and pre-trained ClinicalBERT for report text. Each branch is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. The learned representations are concatenated and passed through two fully connected layers (512 → 256 → 1 logit). Training uses binary cross-entropy loss.

**Baseline:** A CNN-only model using the identical ViT-B or ResNet-50 architecture, trained on image pixels alone, with no access to report text. The baseline receives the same data augmentation, preprocessing, and tuning effort as the vision-language model, ensuring that any performance gap reflects the contribution of multimodal learning, not differential optimization.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC on the test set.** AUROC is chosen because it is threshold-invariant, robust to label imbalance (~10% urgent studies), and interpretable as the probability the model correctly ranks an urgent study above a routine one. This directly aligns with the clinical objective of improving discrimination at any alert volume.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Sensitivity at 95% specificity, computed using the validation-set threshold
- Positive predictive value (PPV) at the same threshold, estimating alert burden
- Per-site AUROC, to detect site-level heterogeneity
- Calibration via Hosmer-Lemeshow test and calibration plots

### 6. Model Selection and Test-Set Policy

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set, and the model achieving the highest AUROC on validation is selected. Five-fold cross-validation within the training set estimates the generalization gap and informs regularization. The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once at the end; no threshold tuning, metric exploration, or post-hoc adjustment occurs on test data.

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Temporal split** isolates learned patterns from scanner drift and report-style evolution
- **Site stratification and per-site AUROC** flag site-level bias
- **Metadata removal** prevents the model from exploiting scanner or timing signals
- **Report feature curation** (excluding final diagnosis) reduces label leakage
- **Adjudicated labels** quantify label noise without biasing the main pipeline
- **Baseline parity** ensures differences reflect multimodal contribution
- **Ablation studies** isolate the text contribution and test robustness to held-out sites

This design would establish that vision-language models outperform CNN baselines on urgent radiograph triage in a temporally coherent, site-diverse setting, assuming the observed gain generalizes beyond the training and validation distributions.
